scenarios.task: Company officer here. First-arriving engine at a two-story single-family home, built 1975 (dimensional lumber, not engineered). On arrival: light smoke showing from a second-floor window, no visible flame, neighbor says everyone's out and accounted for, no reported victims. My driver wants to vent the second-floor window right away to 'let the heat out' before we even get a line charged. Good call?
criteria: Rejects venting before the attack line is charged and in position, naming the uncoordinated-flow-path mechanism (venting adds oxygen/fuels the fire and increases heat release toward the opening) rather than just 'be careful'
Notes that with no confirmed viable rescue, the tactical priority shifts toward attack/exposure protection over a search-driven VEIS entry, per RECEO-VS priority ordering
Recommends coordinating any ventilation timing explicitly with when the line is confirmed charged and flowing, not on a fixed time estimate
Distinguishes this dimensional-lumber 1975 construction from engineered lumber, but still calls for reassessment rather than assuming a long collapse/flashover window by default
States the strategy call (offensive/transitional/defensive) explicitly rather than leaving it implicit

scenarios.task: Reported residential fire, occupant possibly trapped. We're four minutes out per dispatch, structure built 2015 with engineered I-joist floor and roof systems per the permit record. On arrival I need to brief my crew fast on the collapse-time math before we commit. What's the read, given we don't know how long it's actually been burning before the 911 call came in?
criteria: States that engineered I-joist/lightweight construction fails in a fraction of the time dimensional lumber does — collapse clock measured in single-digit minutes rather than 18+ minutes
Explicitly treats unknown pre-arrival burn time as 'assume closer to flashover/collapse than comfortable,' not as a default early-stage assumption

Applies (or references) the rule that two of {engineered/lightweight construction, worsening smoke, unknown duration >10 min} together tighten the SCBA trigger and shorten the reassessment interval from 5 to 3 minutes
Recommends transmitting construction type/era in the initial size-up radio report explicitly, not just occupancy or smoke conditions
Frames this as continuous reassessment (checking again in minutes) rather than a single go/no-go decision made once at the curb

scenarios.task: My firefighter Nguyen just started her interior search at 08:14, air-start on a 4,500 psi/30-min rated cylinder. It's now 08:22, she just found zero visibility with rising heat and hasn't checked in for over 90 seconds. What am I doing right now as the officer outside?
criteria: Flags the missed check-in (>60s) combined with zero visibility/rising heat as a mayday-developing pattern, not just a routine delay

Calculates working-exertion air yield (~13-15 min against the 30-min rating, i.e. roughly half) and notes ~8 minutes elapsed is approaching or past the two-thirds mark, which should already be triggering an exit
Recommends an immediate radio check ('Command to [unit], radio check — location and air status, over') rather than waiting longer
States that if contact isn't reestablished or trouble is confirmed, the next step is treating it as an undeclared mayday and preparing/committing RIT rather than continuing to wait
Does not suggest venting or opening additional points to 'help' her, recognizing that would create an uncoordinated opening/flow path risk

scenarios.task: Mayday just came over the radio from one of my crew — he transmitted 'Mayday mayday mayday, second floor, Bravo side, lost contact with the hoseline, air's getting low' but didn't say his name or unit. Meanwhile my incident commander is still coordinating a second-due engine's water supply on the same channel. Walk me through exactly what should happen in the next 60 seconds.
criteria: States command must immediately clear all non-mayday radio traffic on that channel before anything else proceeds

Notes the transmitted mayday was incomplete LUNAR (missing Unit and Name) and states the firefighter or last-contact crew member should be prompted to complete it, since 'resources needed' and identifying info are what let command actually deploy help
Directs immediate activation/commitment of the staged RIT/FAST crew to the reported location (second floor, Bravo side)
Calls for a PAR (personnel accountability report) of all other crews on scene immediately, to confirm no second incident is developing unnoticed

Notes that other crews not directly supporting the mayday continue their assigned tasks unless redirected, rather than everyone abandoning fire attack (since an uncontrolled fire risks a second mayday)
